title: Dog Open Sore Not Healing: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your dog's sore not healing? Learn about lick granulomas, infections, and why persistent wounds require a medium-urgency veterinary consultation.
content_html:
What is a Non-Healing Open Sore?

An open sore that refuses to heal, also known as a chronic wound or a persistent lesion, is a sign that your dog's natural healing process is being interrupted. These sores can range from raw, red patches to deep ulcers or crusty growths. Common causes include 'lick granulomas' (caused by obsessive licking), bacterial or fungal infections, foreign bodies trapped under the skin, or in some cases, cutaneous tumors or cancers. Because the skin is the body's first line of defense, a breach that stays open leaves your pet vulnerable to secondary complications.

Urgency Level: Medium

A non-healing sore is classified as a medium-urgency situation. While it is rarely a 'stop everything and rush to the ER' emergency—unless the wound is arterial, bleeding uncontrollably, or accompanied by extreme lethargy—it does require a veterinary appointment within the next 24 to 48 hours. Chronic inflammation can lead to deep-tissue infections (cellulitis) or permanent scarring, and if the cause is a growth, early intervention is critical for a better prognosis.

How a Photo Assists in Triage

When you contact a veterinary professional, providing a clear, well-lit photo of the sore is incredibly helpful. A visual allows a triage assistant to assess the wound's margins, look for signs of 'proud flesh' (over-granulation), identify the presence of discharge (pus or serosanguinous fluid), and determine the level of inflammation in the surrounding tissue. These visual cues help us prioritize your pet's appointment and provide immediate first-aid advice, such as whether or not to apply a bandage or an Elizabethan collar to prevent further self-trauma.

faqs.question: Can I put Neosporin on my dog's open sore?
faqs.answer: You should consult your vet before applying any topical ointments. Some ingredients can be toxic if licked off, and ointments may trap bacteria inside a wound that needs to breathe or drain.

faqs.question: Why does my dog keep licking the sore?
faqs.answer: Dogs often lick wounds because of the soothing effect of saliva or due to the itchiness of healing tissue; however, the bacteria in their mouth and the abrasive texture of their tongue usually prevent the wound from closing.

faqs.question: When should a sore be considered an emergency?
faqs.answer: If the sore is associated with a high fever, a foul odor, rapid spreading, or if your dog is refusing to eat, you should seek immediate veterinary care.
